flashcards.front: What is a pointer to a derived type in C++?
flashcards.back: A pointer declared with a derived class type that can hold addresses of objects of that derived class or any of its base classes (upcasting).

flashcards.front: What is upcasting in C++ inheritance?
flashcards.back: Converting a derived class pointer/reference to a base class pointer/reference. It is always safe and implicit in C++.
Converting a base class pointer/reference to a derived class pointer/reference. Requires explicit casting and is unsafe without runtime type checking.

flashcards.front: What is runtime polymorphism in C++?
flashcards.back: Achieved through virtual functions, allowing the derived class version to be called even when the object is accessed through a base class pointer.
They ensure proper cleanup of derived class objects when deleted through a base class pointer, calling destructors in correct order (derived then base).
When a derived class object is assigned to a base class object by value, the derived part is 'sliced off', losing polymorphic behavior.

flashcards.front: What is a virtual function table (vtable)?
flashcards.back: A runtime data structure used by C++ to support dynamic dispatch. Each class with virtual functions has its own vtable containing pointers to virtual function implementations.
A hidden pointer passed to all non-static member functions that points to the object on which the function is called.

flashcards.front: What is a pure virtual function?
flashcards.back: A virtual function with '= 0' syntax that must be overridden by derived classes. Classes with pure virtual functions become abstract classes.
A class containing at least one pure virtual function. Cannot be instantiated directly; used as a base class to define an interface.
A type-safe casting operator that performs runtime type checking. Returns nullptr (C++) or throws exception (references) on failure.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between virtual and non-virtual function calls?
flashcards.back: Virtual functions use dynamic binding (resolved at runtime based on actual object type), while non-virtual functions use static binding (resolved at compile time).

flashcards.front: What is late binding in C++?
flashcards.back: Also called dynamic binding, it defers function resolution until runtime, enabling polymorphic behavior through virtual functions.

flashcards.front: What is an interface in C++?
flashcards.back: A class with all pure virtual functions that defines a contract. Abstract classes serve as interfaces in C++.

flashcards.front: What is the purpose of the 'override' specifier?
flashcards.back: Ensures that a derived class function overrides a virtual function in the base class. Compiler error if no matching virtual function exists.
